schools.summary: The house is Peter's, now Christ's home (Mark 1:29); Augustine harmonises 'his own city' with Capernaum (Matthew 9:1).
Bede: he loosed the sins before the joints, calling the despised man 'son'; how each one's faith weighs with God; Pseudo-Jerome reads the palsy as the soul's torpor.
Theophylact and Chrysostom: he proved the cure of the soul by that of the body, the invisible by the visible.
Henry: the crowd gathered straightway — improve opportunities for the soul without losing time.
Mark's graphic eyewitness detail of the packed house and choked doorway; 'preached the word' = the doctrine of the kingdom (Acts 6:2-7); the descriptive imperfect 'was speaking'.
Wesley: the general impression still held — all received the word with joy before the scribes' opposition.
Mark's vivid historical present 'they come' and the detail 'borne of four' (only in Mark; cf. Luke 5:18); the man too feeble to come alone.
Gill: the company were the sick man's friends and relations.
The flat eastern roof reached by outside stair; digging out the tiles (Luke 5:19); the 'bed' is the poor man's grabatus (John 5:8; Acts 9:33); Lightfoot's 'way of the roof'.
The faith was that of the four and the man himself (Robertson); the astonishing thing is that Jesus forgave sins instead of healing; 'Son' a word of consolation (Ephesians 2:8).
Gill: the faith of the sick man in being brought and of the friends in breaking through every difficulty.
A Markan picture through Peter's eyes — scribes come from every quarter (Luke 5:17, 21) to pick flaws, jealous of Christ's power; their unspoken murmur read by Jesus.
Wesley: the first offence came not from the plain people but from the learned 'blind guides'.
The scribes' logic was sound — only God forgives sins (Isaiah 43:25; Daniel 9:9) — the flaw being their refusal to see Christ's divine relation; the issue is the deity of Christ (Robertson).
Gill: taking Christ for a mere man, they reasoned he blasphemed in claiming what is peculiar to God; Lightfoot's rabbinic confirmation.
Ellicott: 'in his spirit' is peculiar to Mark, bearing on the reality of Christ's human nature.
Gill: he knew their thoughts by his divine nature, which searches the most secret hearts.
Lightfoot: 'easier' means the plainer thing — forgiveness none can test, healing all can see; Christ chooses the visible to prove the invisible.
Gill: the question proves his deity — he who cures the palsy by a word can forgive sins.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): Jesus the Healer ministers first to the soul, then extends healing to the body.
Robertson: the visible healing proves the Son of Man's present authority to forgive (Acts 5:31); the parenthesis identical in all three Synoptics shows Mark's priority.
Gill: though thought a mere man, Christ affirms his authority as the Son of man (Daniel 7:13).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A Passion-meditation on the Son of man, not on this verse.

schools.summary: Gill: Christ bids the man authoritatively to rise and carry his bed publicly, that all might witness the cure.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): the Healer ministers first to the soul ('Sin no more') then the body ('Take up thy bed').
The instant public cure silenced the scribes and amazed all ('We never saw it on this fashion', John 7:31); Christ made good his claim to equality with God.
Clarke: as the palsy is the soul's helplessness, grace repairs all in a moment; Wesley: even the scribes were amazed for a time.
Teaching by the sea near Capernaum (Mark 1:16); Levi identified with Matthew the Evangelist (Matthew 9:9).
Bede: Mark/Luke spare the old name while Matthew accuses himself, that none converted should despair; Pseudo-Jerome: Levi 'Appointed' quits all to follow (Luke 14:33).
Theophylact: the more one flees glory the more she pursues.
Henry: wherever Christ's doctrine is preached, even in corners, we must follow it.
Levi = Matthew (two names), at the toll-gate collecting for Herod Antipas (Matthew 9:9); the sudden call and prompt obedience an object lesson against delay.
Clarke: a Jew in the odious office of tax-gatherer; Gill: called as Christ went toward the sea.
Levi's feast in honour of the Saviour (Luke 5:29); 'sinners' of abandoned character; the 'many' following proves Christ's early popularity.
Wesley: the publicans partly invited by Matthew for compassion, the rest pressing in, drawn by Christ's gracious words.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry concerns 'Christians' named at Antioch (Acts 11:26), tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: On the naming of Christians at Antioch, not on this verse.

schools.summary: 'The scribes of the Pharisees' scandalised that a Jew ate with publicans regarded as Gentiles (Acts 11:3); Lightfoot's rabbinic list of 'sinners'; Clarke's textual variant.
Wesley: the learned raise prejudices, but Christ answers with calm, dispassionate reasoning.
The physician goes to the sick; Jesus accepts for argument the Pharisees' claim to be righteous; 'to repentance' not genuine in Mark (cf. Luke 5:32); Mark omits the Hosea 6:6 citation.
Wesley: if righteous he would not call them — they are the very persons he came to save (1 Timothy 1:15).
Levi's feast fell on a fast-day; John's ascetic disciples (Matthew 11:18) side with the Pharisees (who fasted twice weekly, Luke 18:12), though without real congeniality.
Bede and Augustine: John's disciples kept imperfect Jewish customs; the Bridegroom brings rest, the sons of the bride-chamber fast not while he is present.
Theophylact/Pseudo-Chrysostom: Christ the Bridegroom betrothing the Church; they will fast when he is taken away, longing for his coming.
Henry: strict professors make their own practice a standard and condemn others.
The sons of the bridechamber are the guests; Jesus adopts the Baptist's metaphor (John 3:29), claiming to be the divine Bridegroom of the OT (Hosea 2:19); mourning unfits a wedding.
Cyril of Jerusalem: he who gave wine at Cana for the children of the bride-chamber gives his body and blood.
The prediction of fasting after Christ's departure (Acts 13:2); Clarke's variant 'in that day' pointing to his being delivered up.
Gill: already a mourning time for John's disciples, their master imprisoned.
The rare verb 'seweth on' (only here); the raw, unshrunk cloth that tears the old garment (Matthew 9:16; Luke 5:36).
Gill: the old garment is the traditions of the elders, onto which the gospel cannot be patched.
The 'bottles' are skins; new fermenting wine bursts old skins, so it needs fresh ones (Matthew 9:17; Luke 5:38); the closing clause bracketed by some but genuine in Luke.
Gill: the old bottles are the self-satisfied Pharisees; the new wine the love of God and the gospel they cannot receive.
The hungry disciples plucked grain as the Law allowed (Deuteronomy 23:25); the charge was the 'work' of preparing food, not theft; poverty is not disgraceful (Barnes).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: freed from the figure, they keep not the figurative sabbath; Augustine: the Law allowed plucking another's corn, so the charge was sabbath-breaking.
Henry: the sabbath is a privilege and benefit, not a task and drudgery, never meant to be a burden.
The Cyril of Jerusalem note here concerns Antioch, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: An Antioch passage, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The plucking was lawful by Moses without distinction of days; the Pharisees denied it on the sabbath, but Christ shows from their own law it was not unlawful.
Calvin: they blame not theft but sabbath-violation, as if men should rather die than eat; the Law must be read by the Legislator's design — superstition joined to hatred.
Christ answers from Scripture (Matthew 12:3; 1 Samuel 21:6), citing David's eating of the showbread — an argument from the greater to the less.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry meditates on the garden of burial and the true Vine, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A meditation on Christ's burial and the Vine, not on this verse.

schools.summary: The crux: 1 Samuel 21:1 names Ahimelech, not Abiathar; solutions — Mark names the more famous son, soon high priest (1 Samuel 23:6); both bore both names (2 Samuel 8:17); or 'in the time of Abiathar'.
Gill reads the showbread spiritually; David came on the sabbath when the loaves were renewed.
Wesley: Abimelech was high priest then; the phrase means 'in the time of Abiathar, afterward high priest'.

debates.question: Why does Mark say 'in the days of Abiathar' when Ahimelech was priest (1 Samuel 21:1)?

sides.position: Mark names the more famous Abiathar, present and soon high priest
Both father and son bore both names
'In the time of Abiathar' / leave it unexplained

schools.summary: Mark alone has this saying subordinating the sabbath to man's welfare against rabbinic rules; the sabbath a kind provision for rest and worship; institutionalism the perennial battle.
Calvin: those judge amiss who turn the sabbath to man's ruin, yet it is not abolished but rightly used; Wesley: it gives way to man's necessity.

debates.question: Does 'the sabbath was made for man' abolish the sabbath?

sides.position: No — it teaches the proper use, not abolition

schools.summary: Mark's emphatic 'even' shows the great claim — the Son of Man is master, not slave, of the sabbath, to interpret not abolish (Ephesians 1:22; Revelation 1:10).
Wesley: the supreme Lawgiver may dispense with his own law; Clarke warns against reading 'man' generally, lest the sabbath's obligation be annihilated.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse.

debates.question: Is 'the Son of man' here Christ or man in general?

sides.position: Christ, the Messiah, claiming lordship of the sabbath
Man in general (rejected, as annihilating the sabbath)

outliers.note: A Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse.

themes: Son of man Lord of the sabbath
